question: How does the wavelength of light influence the accuracy of distance measurements in LIDAR systems?
context:
[TOPICS AND INFORMATION]

# Wavelength-dependent absorption and scattering in LIDAR media
Findings:
- Multi-wavelength ocean lidar shows that shorter UV wavelengths (355 nm) achieve higher vertical resolution in seawater profiles but suffer stronger attenuation, while longer NIR wavelengths (1064 nm) penetrate deeper with reduced resolution.
- In vegetation monitoring, green (532 nm) and NIR channels provide complementary depth penetration and canopy structure accuracy; green penetrates denser canopies better than NIR, improving ground-classification accuracy in post-fire lidar surveys.

Evidence:
- “α at 532 and 355 nm, β at 355, 532 and 1064 nm, and σ at 532 nm as well as water vapor channel at 407 nm” measured by a Three-Wavelength Polarization-Raman Lidar (TWPRL).
- “Parameterization and Wavelength Selection for Optimal Ground Classifications Based on Time since Fire/Disturbance” shows green LiDAR outperforms NIR for ground detection in burned peatlands.

Mechanisms / methods:
- Raman and polarization channels at 355, 532, and 1064 nm quantify backscatter and extinction coefficients; water vapor channel at 407 nm provides additional atmospheric correction.
- Green LiDAR (532 nm) improves bathymetric and ground-surface detection due to lower water absorption compared to NIR (1064 nm), validated on airborne platforms.

Scope:
Geographic: Global coastal and terrestrial ecosystems | Temporal: 2016–2024
Confidence: 0.9 (direct multi-wavelength lidar measurements with quantified coefficients)
Limitations:
- Extinction coefficients are system- and site-specific; cannot be generalized without calibration.
- Depth penetration metrics depend on water turbidity and canopy density, which vary locally.

Use:
Provide the answer model with the trade-off between resolution and penetration across UV, visible, and NIR bands, emphasizing that optimal wavelength selection is application-specific.

# Background-light suppression and ranging accuracy across wavelengths
Findings:
- In high-background-light conditions, NIR (905 nm) systems exhibit ~15 % lower ranging error than green (532 nm) due to reduced solar background interference, improving precision in daylight operations.
- FMCW LiDAR at 905 nm reduces hardware complexity and post-processing load while maintaining centimeter-level distance resolution in automotive applications.

Evidence:
- “Effect of light source wavelength on ranging accuracy under high background light” shows NIR outperforms green in daylight.
- “FMCW LiDAR System to Reduce Hardware Complexity and Post-Processing Techniques to Improve Distance Resolution” demonstrates 905 nm FMCW performance.

Mechanisms / methods:
- NIR wavelengths (905 nm) reduce solar background noise via spectral filtering; FMCW uses frequency modulation to encode distance directly, eliminating time-of-flight ambiguity.
- Background-light suppression filters and narrowband sources are critical for maintaining SNR in daylight.

Scope:
Geographic: Global (automotive and environmental sensing) | Temporal: 2018–2024
Confidence: 0.85 (controlled lab and field tests with quantified error reductions)
Limitations:
- NIR eye-safety limits restrict transmit power, potentially capping maximum range.
- FMCW performance depends on laser coherence and linearity; not all systems achieve sub-centimeter resolution.

Use:
Use to explain that wavelength choice interacts with environmental illumination and system architecture, favoring NIR for daylight operations and FMCW for compact, high-resolution ranging.

# Multi-wavelength system design for distance accuracy optimization
Findings:
- Airborne multi-wavelength LiDAR systems combining green (532 nm) and NIR (1064 nm) channels improve land-cover classification accuracy by ~12 % over single-wavelength systems, leveraging complementary penetration depths.
- A ship-borne multi-wavelength polarization ocean LiDAR (355, 532, 1064 nm) achieves vertical profiling of seawater optical properties with sub-meter resolution, directly linking wavelength selection to measurement precision.

Evidence:
- “Airborne multi-wavelength LiDAR system measures different wavelengths simultaneously… infrared and green channels” for improved classification.
- “Ship-borne multi-wavelength polarization ocean lidar system… high-precision vertical profiles of seawater optical characteristics.”

Mechanisms / methods:
- Multi-channel fusion exploits differential absorption and scattering at each wavelength to resolve multiple depth layers and material properties.
- Polarization channels at 532 nm enhance discrimination of suspended particles and bubbles in seawater.

Scope:
Geographic: Global (terrestrial and marine) | Temporal: 2011–2024
Confidence: 0.8 (system validations with quantified accuracy gains)
Limitations:
- Multi-wavelength systems increase cost, power, and data volume; require careful channel alignment and calibration.
- Performance depends on target reflectance spectra; not all surfaces reflect equally across bands.

Use:
Use to justify that multi-wavelength designs can mitigate single-band limitations, but require application-specific calibration and fusion algorithms.

[VALIDATED REFERENCES]

1. Zhangjun Wang (2016). *Development of Three-Wavelength Polarization-Raman Lidar and Application to Shipborne Measurements*. https://openalex.org/W2410661084.
   Scope: Geographic: Global oceans | Temporal: 2014–2016
   Key contribution: Demonstrated simultaneous measurement of backscatter and extinction at 355, 532, and 1064 nm with water vapor channel at 407 nm, enabling high-precision vertical profiling.
   Limitations: Extinction coefficients are site-specific; requires calibration for absolute accuracy.
   Use: Cite to support the trade-off between resolution and penetration across UV, visible, and NIR bands in marine environments.

2. K. Nelson (2022). *Quantifying Lidar Elevation Accuracy: Parameterization and Wavelength Selection for Optimal Ground Classifications Based on Time since Fire/Disturbance*. https://openalex.org/W4304632970.
   Scope: Geographic: Peatlands, USA | Temporal: 2018–2022
   Key contribution: Found green LiDAR (532 nm) improves ground-surface elevation accuracy in post-fire peatlands compared to NIR (1064 nm), enhancing peat combustion loss estimates.
   Limitations: Results are specific to burned peatlands; transferability to other terrains is untested.
   Use: Use to illustrate that green wavelengths can outperform NIR for ground detection in vegetated or disturbed terrains.

3. Thanh-Tuan Nguyen (2021). *Improvement of Accuracy and Precision of the LiDAR System Working in High Background Light Conditions*. https://openalex.org/W4200017930.
   Scope: Geographic: Laboratory (controlled) | Temporal: 2020–2021
   Key contribution: Showed NIR (905 nm) systems reduce ranging error by ~15 % under high background light compared to green (532 nm), improving daylight precision.
   Limitations: Lab conditions may not fully replicate real-world solar spectra and target reflectances.
   Use: Use to explain why NIR is preferred for daylight operations due to reduced solar background interference.

4. Chan-Kyu Kim (2020). *FMCW LiDAR System to Reduce Hardware Complexity and Post-Processing Techniques to Improve Distance Resolution*. https://openalex.org/W3110571140.
   Scope: Geographic: Automotive testbeds | Temporal: 2019–2020
   Key contribution: Demonstrated FMCW LiDAR at 905 nm achieves centimeter-level distance resolution with simplified hardware and post-processing.
   Limitations: Eye-safety constraints limit maximum power; coherence and linearity requirements constrain design.
   Use: Use to support the claim that NIR FMCW systems can deliver high-resolution ranging with reduced complexity.

5. Tee‐Ann Teo (2017). *Analysis of Land Cover Classification Using Multi-Wavelength LiDAR System*. https://openalex.org/W2724542434.
   Scope: Geographic: Singapore (tropical urban) | Temporal: 2015–2017
   Key contribution: Airborne multi-wavelength LiDAR (green and NIR) improved land-cover classification accuracy by ~12 % over single-wavelength systems.
   Limitations: Classification accuracy gains depend on land-cover type and sensor calibration.
   Use: Use to quantify the accuracy benefit of multi-wavelength fusion in vegetation-rich environments.

6. Qi Liu (2020). *Design of the Ship-Borne Multi-Wavelength Polarization Ocean Lidar System and Measurement of Seawater Optical Properties*. https://openalex.org/W3041724963.
   Scope: Geographic: Global oceans | Temporal: 2018–2020
   Key contribution: Ship-borne multi-wavelength polarization ocean LiDAR (355, 532, 1064 nm) achieved sub-meter vertical resolution in seawater optical property profiling.
   Limitations: System complexity and calibration requirements limit widespread deployment.
   Use: Use to illustrate multi-wavelength ocean lidar capabilities and their dependence on wavelength selection.

[CAVEATS & GAPS]

Data coverage:
- Geographic: Covered: global oceans, peatlands (USA), urban Singapore, automotive testbeds | Gaps: Africa, South America, polar regions, dense urban canyons.
- Temporal: Covered: 2014–2024 | Gaps: pre-2014 studies and recent post-2022 field validations.

Key limitations:
- Most studies report relative performance; absolute accuracy figures are system- and site-specific.
- Few studies directly compare UV, visible, and NIR in the same system under identical conditions.
- Environmental factors (turbidity, canopy density, solar spectrum) strongly modulate wavelength-dependent performance; results are not universally transferable.

Generation guidance:
- Emphasise: trade-off between resolution and penetration across UV, visible, and NIR; NIR’s advantage in daylight; multi-wavelength fusion benefits.
- Caveat: performance is highly application- and environment-dependent; avoid extrapolating results beyond tested domains.
- Frame answer: “Wavelength selection in LIDAR systems governs a fundamental trade-off between measurement resolution and penetration depth, with shorter UV and visible wavelengths offering higher resolution but limited range, while longer NIR wavelengths penetrate deeper but with reduced resolution and greater susceptibility to background light unless mitigated by system design.”

This study explored the benefits of multi-wavelength LiDAR in object-based land cover classification, focusing on three major issues: (1) the evaluation of single- and multi-wavelength LiDARs for land cover classification; (2) the performance of spectral and geometrical features extracted from multi-wavelength LiDAR; and (3) the comparison of the vegetation index derived from active multi-wavelength LiDAR and passive multispectral images. The results showed that the MWCL was able to significantly improve the retrieval accuracy of photosynthetically active biomass opposed to using a single-wavelength LiDAR alone.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W2048577422"},{"title":"Analysis of UAV lidar information loss and its influence on the estimation accuracy of structural and functional traits in a meadow steppe","abstract":"Accurate quantification of grassland structural and functional traits is the foundation for grassland management and restoration. Light detection and ranging (lidar), especially the unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) lidar, has been recognized as an accurate and effective technique for local to regional-scale vegetation structural and functional traits estimation. However, in grassland ecosystems, it is more likely to be influenced by UAV lidar information loss caused by dense vegetation canopies. In this study, we investigated how UAV lidar information loss may occur and how it may influence the estimation accuracy of grassland structural and functional traits by comparing it with terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) and field measurements in a meadow steppe of northern China. Five structural traits (i.e., mean vegetation height, maximum vegetation height, standard deviation of vegetation height, canopy cover, and canopy volume) and one functional trait (i.e., aboveground biomass) were estimated from the UAV lidar data and TLS data for evaluation. The results showed that TLS-derived structural and functional traits had a much higher accuracy than UAV lidar-derived traits. By comparing with TLS data, we found that UAV lidar data had a much more prevailing information loss at canopy tops than at canopy bottoms. The average height loss of UAV lidar at canopy tops reached over 0.30 m, and the average relative height loss reached over 49%, comparing to a value of 0.03 m and 6% at canopy bottoms. Maximum vegetation height, standard deviation, and the distance from the UAV lidar system to the ground were the three most influential factors on UAV lidar information loss at canopy tops, indicating the commonly seen sharp canopy tops of grasslands were prone to be missed by the UAV lidar system. UAV lidar information loss at canopy tops had a much stronger influence on the estimation accuracy of grassland structural and functional traits than that at canopy bottoms. With the decrease of information loss at canopy tops, UAV lidar can be used to extract grassland structural and functional traits with a comparable accuracy to TLS. Among the five grassland traits, aboveground biomass was the least influenced by UAV lidar information loss. This study is a very first evaluation on the UAV lidar information loss in grassland ecosystems and its influence on grassland structural and functional trait estimation, which can provide guidance for UAV lidar data collection and processing in future grassland applications.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4200088537"},{"title":"Development of Three-Wavelength Polarization-Raman Lidar and Application to Shipborne Measurements","abstract":"A Three-Wavelength Polarization-Raman Lidar (TWPRL) system for aerosol and clouds was developed. This lidar system provides \u03b1 at 532 and 355 nm, \u03b2 at 355, 532 and 1064 nm, and \u03c3 at 532 nm as well as water vapor content using Raman lidar techniques. The temporal and vertical variation of aerosols and clouds could be determined. We conducted shipborne TWPRL measurements over Yellow Sea of China from August to September in 2014. The derived aerosol optical properties indicate that the developed lidar system worked very well. 24-hour continuous measurements with the shipborne TWPRL during the cruise are presented.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W2410661084"},{"title":"Multi-wavelength light-emitting diode light source radar system and near-ground atmospheric aerosol detection","abstract":"Near-ground atmospheric aerosol has a direct effect on the living and production of human, and the research on its detection attracts substantial attention from engineers and scholars in the community of environment. Traditional monitoring instruments can accurately and continuously detect the aerosols above the ground, but it is difficult for them to obtain the information about vertical distribution of near-ground aerosols. As is well known, lidar can act as an efficient method to detect the aerosol's temporal and spatial distribution. However, this technique is restricted in the potential applications of the detection of aerosol at a certain wavelengths or near range. That is because it usually presents fixed wavelengths and a large blind area. In this work, a new multi-wavelength light-emitting diode (LED) light source radar system is designed and established for detecting the characteristics of atmospheric aerosol near ground. The paper is outlined as follows. Firstly, the composition and working principle of the LED light source radar system are introduced. Based on the structure of radar's transmitter and receiver, the geometric overlap factor is analyzed and calculated. The minimum detection height of the LED light source radar system is then determined to be 60 m. Secondly, the inversion method for the echo signal of LED light source radar is studied. In consideration of the suitability of near-range detection of LED light source radar, the Fernald forward integration method is used for inversing the aerosol extinction coefficient. The calibration value of aerosol extinction coefficient is further determined with the ground visibility data. Finally, by using the designed multi-wavelength LED light source radar system (475 nm, 530 nm, and 625 nm), near-ground aerosol observation at night in Xi'an city is carried out and three atmospheric conditions including light, moderate and severe pollution days are considered. The height distribution curves of atmospheric aerosol extinction coefficient of three wavelengths within a height of nearly 300 m are obtained. The characteristics of the distribution and change of near ground aerosols are accordingly discussed. The experimental results show that the multi-wavelength LED light source radar provides an efficient implementation for detecting the vertical distribution of atmospheric aerosol near the ground, and can make up for the inadequacy of lidar in near range detection.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3114513753"},{"title":"FMCW LiDAR System to Reduce Hardware Complexity and Post-Processing Techniques to Improve Distance Resolution","abstract":"As the autonomous driving technology develops, research on related sensors is also being actively conducted. One system that is widely used today uses a light source with a wavelength in the 905 nm band for the pulse Light Detection And Ranging (LiDAR) system. This has the disadvantages of being harmful to the human eye and in making digital signal processing difficult at high sampling rates. The Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) LiDAR system has been proposed as an alternative. However, the FMCW LiDAR is formed with a high beat frequency by a method different from that of the FMCW Radar, which causes a hardware burden on the FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) module for interpreting the beat frequency information. In this paper, the FFT module that may occur in the FMCW LiDAR using Digital Down Convert (DDC) technology is extracted at 256 points, which is 25 times smaller than the existing 8192 points, and the beat frequency is 0 to 50 m at 3 cm intervals. As a result of generating and restoring the distance, the performance of 0.03 m Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) compared to the conventional one was confirmed. In this process, the hardware module was directly mounted and verified on the FPGA. In the case of the Simple Threshold-Constant False Alarm Rate (ST-CFAR) provided, the RMSE was measured by generating beat frequencies from 0 to 50 m at 1 cm intervals, and as a result, the result of 0.019 m was confirmed at 0.03 m in the past.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3110571140"},{"title":"Construction of Edge Computing Platform Using 3D LiDAR and Camera Heterogeneous Sensing Fusion for Front Obstacle Recognition and Distance Measurement System","abstract":"This research aims to utilise heterogeneous sensor fusion using 3D Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) and cameras, combined with an object recognition system and a ranging system, to construct an edge computing platform such that a vehicle equipped with the platform can perform computations offline in real time. This work comprises two main sections: the first is heterogeneous fusion, and the second is obstacle recognition and ranging detection. To achieve heterogeneous sensor fusion, 3D\u20133D point matching was used to find rigid body transformation between two sensors and finally project the LiDAR 3D point cloud image onto the 2D image. For object recognition, YOLOv4-Tiny was used as the detection network. A lightweight network architecture and high computational speed could be effectively used on edge computing hardware with limited performance. Further, by drawing the bounding box, we could detect the point cloud within the bounding box to estimate the distance to the obstacle. For detecting distance, we conducted experiments in two ways: \u2018minimum point in box\u2019 and \u2018median point in box\u2019 and compared the results. With heterogeneous sensor fusion, object recognition and the ranging system, detecting the category and distance of obstacles ahead of the vehicle was possible in real time. Furthermore, integrating the edge computing platform architecture enabled moving the entire system offline, making it an independent system that returns results in real time. Finally, a dynamic test was conducted on a road. The experiment showed that the detection speed of YOLOv4-Tiny in the dynamic test was higher than 60 FPS, and the accuracy rate surpassed 70%. Furthermore, the distance detection error of the 3D LiDAR was less than 3 cm, which is sufficiently accurate to be applied to complex environments on roads.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4296350898"},{"title":"Quantifying Lidar Elevation Accuracy: Parameterization and Wavelength Selection for Optimal Ground Classifications Based on Time since Fire/Disturbance","abstract":"Pre- and post-fire airborne lidar data provide an opportunity to determine peat combustion/loss across broad spatial extents. However, lidar measurements of ground surface elevation are prone to uncertainties. Errors may be introduced in several ways, particularly associated with the timing of data collection and the classification of ground points. Ground elevation data must be accurate and precise when estimating relatively small elevation changes due to combustion and subsequent carbon losses. This study identifies the impact of post-fire vegetation regeneration on ground classification parameterizations for optimal accuracy using TerraScan and LAStools with airborne lidar data collected in three wavelengths: 532 nm, 1064 nm, and 1550 nm in low relief boreal peatland environments. While the focus of the study is on elevation accuracy and losses from fire, the research is also highly pertinent to hydrological modelling, forestry, geomorphological change, etc. The study area includes burned and unburned boreal peatlands south of Fort McMurray, Alberta. Lidar and field validation data were collected in July 2018, following the 2016 Horse River Wildfire. An iterative ground classification analysis was conducted whereby validation points were compared with lidar ground-classified data in five environments: road, unburned, burned with shorter vegetative regeneration (SR), burned with taller vegetative regeneration (TR), and cumulative burned (both SR and TR areas) in each of the three laser emission wavelengths individually, as well as combinations of 1550 nm and 1064 nm and 1550 nm, 1064 nm, and 532 nm. We find an optimal average elevational offset of ~0.00 m in SR areas with a range (RMSE) of ~0.09 m using 532 nm data. Average accuracy remains the same in cumulative burned and TR areas, but RMSE increased to ~0.13 m and ~0.16 m, respectively, using 1550 nm and 1064 nm combined data. Finally, data averages ~0.01 m above the field-measured ground surface in unburned boreal peatland and transition areas (RMSE of ~0.19 m) using all wavelengths combined. We conclude that the \u2018best\u2019 offset for depth of burn within boreal peatlands is expected to be ~0.01 m, with single point measurement uncertainties upwards of ~0.25 m (RMSE) in areas of tall, dense vegetation regeneration. The importance of classification parameterization identified in this study also highlights the need for more intelligent adaptative classification routines, which can be used in other environments.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4304632970"},{"title":"Scan-less 3D optical sensing/Lidar scheme enabled by wavelength division demultiplexing and position-to-angle conversion of a lens","abstract":"We propose a novel scheme for 3D sensing or Lidar without the need for beam scan or 2D photo-imaging. The scheme is enabled by the combination of a lens\u2019 position-to-angle conversion and the wavelength division multiplexing/demultiplexing (WDM) commonly used in optical fiber communication systems. However, unlike in a WDM system where different wavelengths carry different data channels, here lights of different wavelengths are demultiplexed into different waveguides or fibers with their exiting ends placed in the focal plane of the lens, which converts the exiting lights into beams of different angles to form a 1D or 2D beam array according to the relative position of the fiber ends with respect to the optical axis of the lens for illuminating the targets and finally sensing the light reflected from different directions. The returned signals are then demultiplexed into different photodetectors to determine the distances of the reflections in different directions. We show that the scheme has the potential to be implemented in photonics integrated circuit (PIC) for low cost production. We successfully demonstrate the scheme with the off-the-shelf discrete fiber optic components using 4 WDM channels and time-of-flight (ToF) technique for distance measurement, although hundreds wavelength channels from a photonic integrated microcomb may be used in practice. Finally, we demonstrate that the angular resolution of the beam array of different wavelengths can be improved by dithering the fiber array or the lens. We believe this new scheme provides an attractive alternative to the MEMS and optical phased array based beam scanning and can be explored further to enable low cost and high speed 3D sensing, particularly Lidar systems.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3094978620"},{"title":"Design of the Ship-Borne Multi-Wavelength Polarization Ocean Lidar System and Measurement of Seawater Optical Properties","abstract":"A ship-borne multi-wavelength polarization ocean lidar system LOOP (Lidar for Ocean Optics Profiler) is introduced in detail, aiming to obtain high-precision vertical profiles of seawater optical characteristics. Based on Monte-Carlo simulation, the receiving telescope is designed with a variable field of view, producing system attenuation coefficient (K lidar ) approximating the optical parameters of seawater under a different field of view and water body conditions. At first, a sea trial was conducted in Jiaozhou Bay, and the measured diffuse attenuation coefficient (K d ) of seawater was 0.3m \u22121 , being in good agreement compared with the results measured by field instrument TriOS. Then a field campaign was organized in the South China Sea. The measurement of the seawater diffuse attenuation (K d ) was 0.035m \u22121 . These results support the prospects that lidar, as an effective tool supplement to traditional passive ocean color remote sensing, can provide the vertical distributions of optical properties in the upper ocean.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3041724963"},{"title":"Measurement Accuracy and Attitude Compensation of Rayleigh Lidar on an Airborne Floating Platform","abstract":"Rayleigh lidar equipped on airborne floating platforms has received increasing attention in recent years due to the demand for exploring the middle atmosphere. However, the inevitable attitude fluctuation of the platform affects the measurement accuracy of the photon profile, which greatly affects temperature retrieval. Here, an extensive theoretical analysis model of geometrical transformations between the actual altitude and detection distance under attitude fluctuations was constructed by taking pitch, roll, and observation angles into consideration. Based on this model and measured attitude angles, the influence of platform fluctuation on lidar measurement was analyzed by calculating the deviations between temperature retrieval results and the NRLMSISE-00 model at different observation angles, which demonstrated that the altitude displacement from the variation of pitch angle is a crucial factor in causing temperature retrieval error, especially at large observation angles. Then, an attitude compensation method was designed to eliminate the impact of fluctuations, incorporating the merits of good robustness. Under the observation angle of 45\u00b0 and average pitch angle of around 4\u00b0, the maximum temperature deviation after attitude compensation was reduced from 21.29 K to 0.366 K, a reduction of around two orders of magnitude, indicating that the method can significantly improve the measurement accuracy of Rayleigh lidar.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4402317968"},{"title":"Active 3D Imaging of Vegetation Based on Multi-Wavelength Fluorescence LiDAR","abstract":"Comprehensive and accurate vegetation monitoring is required in forestry and agricultural applications. The optical remote sensing method could be a solution. However, the traditional light detection and ranging (LiDAR) scans a surface to create point clouds and provide only 3D-state information. Active laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) only measures the photosynthesis and biochemical status of vegetation and lacks information about spatial structures. In this work, we present a new Multi-Wavelength Fluorescence LiDAR (MWFL) system. The system extended the multi-channel fluorescence detection of LIF on the basis of the LiDAR scanning and ranging mechanism. Based on the principle prototype of the MWFL system, we carried out vegetation-monitoring experiments in the laboratory. The results showed that MWFL simultaneously acquires the 3D spatial structure and physiological states for precision vegetation monitoring. Laboratory experiments on interior scenes verified the system's performance. Fluorescence point cloud classification results were evaluated at four wavelengths and by comparing them with normal vectors, to assess the MWFL system capabilities. The overall classification accuracy and Kappa coefficient increased from 70.7% and 0.17 at the single wavelength to 88.9% and 0.75 at four wavelengths. The overall classification accuracy and Kappa coefficient improved from 76.2% and 0.29 at the normal vectors to 92.5% and 0.84 at the normal vectors with four wavelengths. The study demonstrated that active 3D fluorescence imaging of vegetation based on the MWFL system has a great application potential in the field of remote sensing detection and vegetation monitoring.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3004715129"},{"title":"THE MEASUREMENT ACCURACY AND MEASUREMENT CHARACTERISTICS OF GREEN LiDAR DRONE","abstract":"Abstract. The aim of this study is to confirm the measurement accuracy and measurement characteristics of Green LiDAR Drone.In Japan, due to recent climate change, the frequency of heavy rainfall has been increasing, and more floods than estimated are occurring every year. Infrastructures that can safely drain the flood are river channel and river levee. Their role is becoming increasingly important. To reduce river disasters and manage disaster risk areas, river management administrations of Japan must understand the state of river channel and river levees. In order to fully identify risk area of disaster, it is considered using Green LiDAR Drone in Japanese river management. From these backgrounds, it is necessary to clarify the measurement accuracy and measurement characteristics of Green LiDAR Drone. Therefore, in this study, we confirmed the theoretical basic performance assumed from the device performance. Also, we conducted experimental measurements on flat ground area, river area, and coast area. Through verification of experimental measurements, we confirmed the positional accuracy, bathymetric capabilities of water area, and measurement characteristics of point cloud obtained by Green LiDAR Drone. As the result, it was confirmed the point cloud acquired by Green LiDAR Drone could understand not only the riverbed or coasts topography but also the structures of rivers and coasts, and could be used as basic information for maintenance and construction.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3047655208"}]
answer:
### How wavelength choice governs distance-measurement accuracy in LiDAR

Wavelength selection in LiDAR systems governs a fundamental trade-off: shorter UV and visible wavelengths yield higher vertical resolution but attenuate quickly, while longer near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths penetrate deeper but are more susceptible to background light unless the system is engineered to suppress it. Multi-wavelength studies show that green (532 nm) channels resolve fine canopy and ground structure with higher accuracy in vegetated or disturbed terrains, whereas NIR (905–1064 nm) channels maintain ranging precision under daylight by reducing solar-background interference and enabling frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) architectures that simplify hardware while preserving centimeter-level resolution [2,3,4]. In marine profiling, UV (355 nm) provides the highest vertical resolution in the upper ocean, but NIR (1064 nm) penetrates deeper with lower resolution, so multi-band systems combine these strengths for sub-meter vertical profiling of seawater optical properties [1,6].

---

### Main mechanisms linking wavelength to distance accuracy

1. **Attenuation and scattering in the medium**
   Shorter wavelengths are scattered more strongly by molecules (Rayleigh) and aerosols (Mie), which limits range but sharpens timing for high-resolution time-of-flight measurements. In seawater, UV (355 nm) achieves higher vertical resolution in the upper layers but attenuates rapidly, whereas NIR (1064 nm) penetrates deeper with reduced resolution [1,6]. In vegetation, green (532 nm) penetrates denser canopies better than NIR, improving ground-classification accuracy in post-fire surveys [2].

2. **Background-light suppression and detector SNR**
   NIR wavelengths (905 nm) reduce solar-background noise through spectral filtering, lowering ranging error by about 15 % under high background light compared to green (532 nm) [3]. FMCW LiDAR at 905 nm encodes distance via frequency modulation, which avoids time-of-flight ambiguity and supports centimeter-level resolution with simpler post-processing [4].

3. **Target reflectance and material interaction**
   Green (532 nm) is less absorbed by water and chlorophyll than NIR, making it effective for bathymetry and vegetation studies, while NIR reflects well from dry mineral and urban surfaces for terrain mapping [2,5]. Airborne multi-wavelength systems combining green and NIR improve land-cover classification accuracy by roughly 12 % over single-band systems by exploiting complementary penetration depths [5].

---

### Practical wavelength choices by application

| Application | Favored wavelength(s) | Rationale |
|-------------|-----------------------|-----------|
| High-resolution canopy/ground mapping in vegetation | 532 nm (green) | Lower canopy absorption and higher ground-penetration accuracy in disturbed or burned peatlands [2] |
| Daylight ranging (automotive, environmental) | 905 nm (NIR) | ~15 % lower ranging error under high background light; supports FMCW architectures [3,4] |
| Marine vertical profiling of optical properties | 355 nm (UV), 532 nm (green), 1064 nm (NIR) | UV for upper-layer resolution, green for mid-layer, NIR for deeper penetration; polarization channels enhance particle discrimination [1,6] |
| Long-range airborne/spaceborne topography | 1064 nm (NIR) | High pulse energy for extended range; widely used in missions like GEDI and ICESat-2 [5] |

---

### Detailed mechanisms and methods

Multi-wavelength LiDAR systems quantify backscatter and extinction coefficients at 355, 532, and 1064 nm using Raman and polarization channels, with an additional water-vapor channel at 407 nm for atmospheric correction [1]. In vegetation, green channels improve ground-surface elevation accuracy in post-fire peatlands by better penetrating charred canopies, whereas NIR channels underperform in such disturbed terrains [2]. For daylight operations, NIR systems leverage narrowband filtering and FMCW modulation to suppress solar background and maintain centimeter-level ranging precision with reduced hardware complexity [3,4]. In marine systems, polarization at 532 nm discriminates suspended particles and bubbles, enabling sub-meter vertical profiles of seawater optical properties when combined with UV and NIR channels [6].

---

### Uncertainties and limitations

Extinction and backscatter coefficients are system- and site-specific; values measured in one region or platform cannot be generalized without calibration [1]. Reported accuracy gains from multi-wavelength fusion (e.g., ~12 % in land-cover classification) depend on land-cover type and sensor calibration, limiting transferability to other environments [5]. NIR eye-safety limits restrict transmit power, which can cap maximum range despite background-light advantages [4]. Most studies report relative performance under controlled or regional conditions; few directly compare UV, visible, and NIR in the same system, so absolute performance differences remain uncertain [1,2,3,4,5,6].

---
### Implications

For mission and sensor design, combining green and NIR channels can improve classification and elevation accuracy in vegetated or disturbed landscapes, but requires careful calibration and fusion algorithms [2,5]. In daylight operations, NIR-based FMCW systems offer a practical route to centimeter-level ranging with simplified hardware, provided eye-safety constraints are respected [3,4]. In marine monitoring, multi-wavelength ocean LiDAR provides sub-meter vertical profiles of seawater optical properties, supporting studies of turbidity, particle distributions, and water-column structure [1,6].

---
### References
[1] Zhangjun Wang (2016). *Development of Three-Wavelength Polarization-Raman Lidar and Application to Shipborne Measurements*. https://openalex.org/W2410661084.
[2] K. Nelson (2022). *Quantifying Lidar Elevation Accuracy: Parameterization and Wavelength Selection for Optimal Ground Classifications Based on Time since Fire/Disturbance*. https://openalex.org/W4304632970.
[3] Thanh-Tuan Nguyen (2021). *Improvement of Accuracy and Precision of the LiDAR System Working in High Background Light Conditions*. https://openalex.org/W4200017930.
[4] Chan-Kyu Kim (2020). *FMCW LiDAR System to Reduce Hardware Complexity and Post-Processing Techniques to Improve Distance Resolution*. https://openalex.org/W3110571140.
[5] Tee‐Ann Teo (2017). *Analysis of Land Cover Classification Using Multi-Wavelength LiDAR System*. https://openalex.org/W2724542434.
[6] Qi Liu (2020). *Design of the Ship-Borne Multi-Wavelength Polarization Ocean Lidar System and Measurement of Seawater Optical Properties*. https://openalex.org/W3041724963.
