This study demonstrates the potential of using field-scale soil moisture simulations to support high-resolution agricultural yield prediction and drought monitoring at the smallholder farm field scale. We present a multiscale modeling approach that combines HydroBlocks \u2013 a physically based hyper-resolution land surface model (LSM) \u2013 with machine learning. We used HydroBlocks to simulate root zone soil moisture and soil temperature in Zambia at 3 h 30 m resolution. These simulations, along with remotely sensed vegetation indices, meteorological data, and descriptors of the physical landscape (related to topography, land cover, and soils) were combined with district-level maize data to train a random forest (RF) model to predict maize yields at district and field scales (250 m). Our model predicted yields with an average testing coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.57 and mean absolute error (MAE) of 310 kg ha\u22121 using year-based cross-validation. Our predicted maize losses due to the 2015\u20132016 El Ni\u00f1o drought agreed well with losses reported by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO). Our results reveal that soil moisture is the strongest and most reliable predictor of maize yield, driving its spatial and temporal variability. Soil moisture was also a more effective indicator of drought impacts on crops than precipitation, soil and air temperatures, and remotely sensed normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI)-based drought indices. This study demonstrates how field-scale modeling can help bridge the spatial-scale gap between drought monitoring and agricultural impacts.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3081108599"},{"title":"Enhancing crop yield and conserving soil moisture through mulching practices in dryland agriculture","abstract":"Dryland agriculture requires the efficient utilization of water resources and the implementation of water-conserving technologies. Mulching is a water conservation practice used in arid land areas to preserve soil moisture, control temperature, and minimize soil evaporation rates. Organic mulching minimizes soil deterioration, enhances organic matter, and boosts the soil\u2019s ability to retain water. Mulching can help keep moisture in the root zone, allowing plants to receive water for extended periods. Mulching with composted yard waste led to higher soil nutrient levels, including phosphorus (P), potassium (K), calcium (Ca), and organic matter when compared to uncovered soil. Under plastic mulch, soluble nutrients such as nitrate (NO 3 \u2212 ), ammonium (NH 4 + ), calcium (Ca 2+ ), magnesium (Mg 2+ ), potassium (K + ), and fulvic acid are released as organic matter decomposes, enhancing the soil\u2019s nutrient availability. Mulching has several advantages for dryland agriculture, such as reducing soil water loss, soil erosion, weed growth, water droplet kinetic energy, and competition for nutrients and water with nearby fields. This review article aimed to demonstrate the effectiveness of ground mulching in water conservation. This is particularly important in arid regions where agricultural sustainability is at risk due to drought, heat stress, and the inefficient use of limited water resources during the cropping season. Ground mulching is essential for minimizing surface evaporation and hence decreasing water loss. This review research thoroughly examines the advantages of organic and synthetic mulches in crop production, as well as their use in the preservation of soil and water resources.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4393231321"},{"title":"Modeling Regional Crop Yield and Irrigation Demand Using SMAP Type of Soil Moisture Data","abstract":"Abstract Agricultural models, such as the Decision Support System for Agrotechnology Transfer cropping system model (DSSAT-CSM), have been developed for predicting crop yield at field and regional scales and to provide useful information for water resources management. A potentially valuable input to agricultural models is soil moisture. Presently, no observations of soil moisture exist covering the entire United States at adequate time (daily) and space (~10 km or less) resolutions desired for crop yield assessments. Data products from NASA\u2019s upcoming Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) mission will fill the gap. The objective of this study is to demonstrate the usefulness of the SMAP soil moisture data in modeling and forecasting crop yields and irrigation amount. A simple, efficient data assimilation algorithm is presented in which the agricultural crop model DSSAT-CSM is constrained to produce modeled crop yield and irrigation amounts that are consistent with SMAP-type data. Numerical experiments demonstrate that incorporating the SMAP data into the agricultural model provides an added benefit of reducing the uncertainty of modeled crop yields when the weather input data to the crop model are subject to large uncertainty.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W2167135978"},{"title":"Multi\u2010Decadal Soil Moisture and Crop Yield Variability\u2014A Case Study With the Community Land Model (CLM5)","abstract":"Abstract While the impacts of climate change on global food security have been studied extensively, the capability of emerging tools that couple land surface processes and crop growth in reproducing inter\u2010annual yield variability at regional scale remains to be tested rigorously. In this study, we analyzed the effects of weather variations between years (1999\u20132019) on regional crop productivity for two agriculturally managed regions with contrasting climate and cropping conditions: the German state of North Rhine\u2010Westphalia (DE\u2010NRW) and the Australian state of Victoria (AUS\u2010VIC), using the latest version of the Community Land Model (CLM5) and the WFDE5 (WATCH Forcing Data methodology applied to ECMWF reanalysis version 5) reanalysis. Overall, the simulation results were able to reproduce the total annual crop yields of certain crops, while also capturing the differences in total yield magnitudes between the domains. However, the simulations showed limitations in correctly capturing inter\u2010annual differences of crop yield compared to official yield records, which resulted in relatively low correlation coefficients between 0.07 and 0.39 in AUS\u2010VIC and between 0.11 and 0.42 in DE\u2010NRW. The mean absolute deviation of simulated winter wheat yields was up to 4.6 times lower compared to state\u2010wide records from 1999 to 2019. Our results suggest the following limitations of CLM5: (a) limitations in simulating yield responses from plant hydraulic stress; (b) errors in simulating soil moisture contents compared to satellite\u2010derived data; and (c) errors in the representation of cropland in general, for example, crop parameterizations and human influences.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4402566589"},{"title":"Comparing quasi-3D soil moisture derived from electromagnetic induction with 1D moisture sensors and correlation to barley yield in variable duplex soil","abstract":"Farms in Western Australia (WA) are highly variable in soil texture and water retention capacity; therefore, information of soil moisture spatial variability in the field is important for effective crop management. In practice, farmers often rely on point sensors to determine soil moisture in their fields for crop planning. The limitation of point measurements to account for spatial variability highlights the need to develop methods that can assess soil moisture across variable broadacre fields. This information can enable more effective site-specific crop management practices. In this study, we used a mobile non-intrusive electromagnetic induction (EMI) sensor to map soil apparent electrical conductivity (ECa) to predict soil moisture levels at three different depths (0\u20130.5, 0.5\u20130.8 and 0.8\u20131.6 m) across the field and compared it with barley yield production. To convert the depth specific electrical conductivity (EC) from EMI surveys into soil moisture estimates, calibrations between electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) to volumetric moisture were used. These calibrations were developed for the different soil textural classes of the field, with R2 of 0.97\u20130.99. There was no significant difference between the estimated soil moisture from EMI surveys and the point moisture measurements obtained from soil moisture sensors and soil sample extracts, with Pearson R values of 0.64 for 0\u20130.5 m depth and 0.73 for 0.5\u20130.8 m depth. Additionally, correcting the temperature drift relative to the measured soil temperature on EMI surveys by 10% in dry season and 31% in wet season, improved the moisture estimation results. This study successfully demonstrated the effectiveness of spatial soil moisture estimation using EMI sensor in a field characterized by horizontal and vertical soil texture variability. However, no significant correlation was found between the mapped soil moisture or soil texture with barley yield. This may be due to relatively high initial moisture levels resulting from two years of fallow rotation.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4388743126"},{"title":"Effect of Raised Flat Bed and Ridge Planting on Wheat Crop Growth and Yield under Varying Soil Moisture Depletions","abstract":"Crop yields remain significantly low in underdeveloped countries, such as Pakistan, primarily due to the prevalent use of traditional farming methods by most farmers. Resource-saving strategies, like ridge and raised flat bed systems, could boost water use efficiency and production per acre. However, further research is needed to investigate the effectiveness of these methods, along with different levels of soil moisture depletion (SMD), on wheat development in the climatic conditions of Sindh Province. Thus, field trials were undertaken at Sindh Agriculture University\u2019s Tandojam for two years (2022 and 2023) during the wheat growing seasons. The experiment included six treatments, viz., T1 (raised flat bed method under 40% depletion), T2 (ridge method under 40% depletion), T3 (raised flat bed method under 50% depletion), T4 (ridge method under 50% depletion), T5 (raised flat bed method under 60% depletion), and T6 (ridge method under 60% depletion). The bed planter was employed to make raised flat beds, and the ridges were prepared using a ridge planter. Wheat variety (TJ-83) was sown. Under all treatments, the plant population, plant height, and seed index were statistically significant (p < 0.05), although spike length, grain spikes\u22121, and grain weight spikes\u22121 were non-significant at different SMD levels. To compute water saving through the ridge and raised flat methods, the recommended delta value for wheat crops under the traditional method was used as a benchmark. Both irrigation technologies conserve water compared to traditional irrigation methods. The T5 treatment conserved the most water, followed by T6. Under a 60% depletion level, the ridge approach produced the highest yield of 2175 kg ha\u22121, compared to 601 kg ha\u22121 with the raised flat bed method. The best crop water productivity (CWP) of 1.34 kg m\u22123 was achieved under T6, whereas raised flat beds attained the lowest CWP of 0.27 kg m\u22123 under T1. In terms of wheat grain production and CWP, the ridge furrow method outperformed the raised flat bed furrow method. Based on the results, it is recommended that farmers should use the ridge furrow method and allow a 60% depletion of soil moisture to obtain a high yield of wheat crops.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4400213620"},{"title":"Soil Fertility Level and Cropping Practices Determining Soybean Yield in Northern East and Center of Benin","abstract":"Soybean is a food security crop in Benin due to its high nutritional value but its yield in the farmers\u2019 cropping system is very low. The present study aims to provide appropriate response to the yield variability among fields in two agro-ecological zones of Benin namely: Southern Borgou zone (AEZ 3 in the north) and cotton zone of central Benin (AEZ 5). Soil samples were collected from 0-20 cm depth in 120 fields (50 in the AEZ 3 and 70 in the AEZ 5). pH (water), soil organic carbon (Walkley and Black method), total nitrogen (Kjeldahl method), CEC (0.01 N ammonium acetate at pH 7 method) and available phosphorus (Bray 1) were determined in the laboratory of Soil Science Water and Environment (LSSEE) of the National Agricultural Research Institute of Benin (INRAB). Cropping system (crop rotations, soil fertility management practices) were also collected using an open ended questionnaire. Classification and regression trees (CARTs) models were used for data analyses. Soybean yield variability among the agro-ecological zones were registered and the highest yield recorded was less than 1 t.ha-1. Considering soil characteristics, soil organic matter level was the most important variable determining yield variability. Furthermore, quantities of P applied and farmyard manure were cropping practices inducing yield variability (86.4% and 15% of the variability respectively). Our results also show that, yield differences noticed among the agro-ecological zones were induced by CEC and pH (water). The study suggested promotion of integrated soil fertility management practices to sustain soybean yield in the study area.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W2989898473"},{"title":"A Comparison between Variable Deficit Irrigation and Farmers\u2019 Irrigation Practices under Three Fertilization Levels in Cotton Yield (Gossypium hirsutum L.) Using Precision Agriculture, Remote Sensing, Soil Analyses, and Crop Growth Modeling","abstract":"The major global challenge for the coming decades will be increasing crop production with less water consumption. Precision agriculture (PA) and variable deficit irrigation (VDI) are management strategies that help farmers to improve crop production, fertilizer\u2019s efficiency, and water use efficiency (WUE). The effects of irrigation (IR1 = variable deficit irrigation; IR2 = farmers\u2019 irrigation common practices) under three fertilization (Ft1, Ft2, Ft3) treatments were studied on a cotton yield, on various indicators for more efficient water and fertilizer use, and on plant growth characteristics by applying a number of new agrotechnologies (such as TDR sensors; soil moisture (SM); PA; remote-sensing NDVI (Sentinel-2 satellite sensors); soil hydraulic analyses; geostatistical models; and SM root-zone modelling 2D GIS mapping). The reference evapotranspiration was computed based on the F.A.O. Penman\u2013Monteith method. The crop (ETc) and actual (ETa) evapotranspiration were computed using crop coefficients obtained from the remote-sensing NDVI vegetation index (R2 = 0.9327). A daily soil\u2013water\u2013crop\u2013atmosphere (SWCA) balance model and a depletion model were developed using sensor data (climatic parameters\u2019 sensors, as well as soil and satellite sensors) measurements. The two-way ANOVA statistical analysis results revealed that irrigation (IR1 = best) and fertilization treatments (Ft2 = best) significantly affected the cotton yield, the plant height, the plant stem, the boll weight, the above-ground dry matter, nitrogen and fertilizer efficiency, and WUE. VDI, if applied wisely during critical growth stages, could result in a substantial improvement in the yield (up to +28.664%) and water savings (up to 24.941%), thus raising water productivity (+35.715% up to 42.659%), WUE (from farmers\u2019 0.421\u20130.496 kg\u00b7m\u22123 up to a VDI of 0.601\u20130.685 kg\u00b7m\u22123), nitrogen efficiency (+16.888% up to +22.859%), and N-P-K fertilizer productivity (from farmers\u2019 16.754\u201323.769 up to a VDI of 20.583\u201327.957).","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4293727943"},{"title":"Impact of Soil Moisture Data Characteristics on the Sensitivity to Crop Yields Under Drought and Excess Moisture Conditions","abstract":"Soil moisture is often considered a direct way of quantifying agricultural drought since it is a measure of the availability of water to support crop growth. Measurements of soil moisture at regional scales have traditionally been sparse, but advances in land surface modelling and the development of satellite technology to indirectly measure surface soil moisture has led to the emergence of a number of national and global soil moisture data sets that can provide insight into the dynamics of agricultural drought. Droughts are often defined by normal conditions for a given time and place; as a result, data sets used to quantify drought need a representative baseline of conditions in order to accurately establish a normal. This presents a challenge when working with earth observation data sets which often have very short baselines for a single instrument. This study assessed three soil moisture data sets: a surface satellite soil moisture data set from the Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity (SMOS) mission operating since 2010; a blended surface satellite soil moisture data set from the European Space Agency Climate Change Initiative (ESA-CCI) that has a long history and a surface and root zone soil moisture data set from the Canadian Meteorology Centre (CMC)\u2019s Regional Deterministic Prediction System (RDPS). An iterative chi-squared statistical routine was used to evaluate each data set\u2019s sensitivity to canola yields in Saskatchewan, Canada. The surface soil moisture from all three data sets showed a similar temporal trend related to crop yields, showing a negative impact on canola yields when soil moisture exceeded a threshold in May and June. The strength and timing of this relationship varied with the accuracy and statistical properties of the data set, with the SMOS data set showing the strongest relationship (peak X2 = 170 for Day of Year 145), followed by the ESA-CCI (peak X2 = 89 on Day of Year 129) and then the RDPS (peak X2 = 65 on Day of Year 129). Using short baseline soil moisture data sets can produce consistent results compared to using a longer data set, but the characteristics of the years used for the baseline are important. Soil moisture baselines of 18\u201320 years or more are needed to reliably estimate the relationship between high soil moisture and high yielding years. For the relationship between low soil moisture and low yielding years, a shorter baseline can be used, with reliable results obtained when 10\u201315 years of data are available, but with reasonably consistent results obtained with as few as 7 years of data. This suggests that the negative impacts of drought on agriculture may be reliably estimated with a relatively short baseline of data.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W2912601201"},{"title":"Can Soil Moisture and Crop Production Be Influenced by Different Cropping Systems?","abstract":"The different conditions of soil vegetation cover combined with irrigation management and/or agricultural production systems can influence soil moisture content and crop yields. This study investigated the impact of agricultural production systems and center pivot irrigation management on soil moisture content during the cultivation of soybean and off-season corn crops. Two field experiments were conducted during the 2018\u20132019 growing season in tropical Cerrado soil conditions; one experiment consisted of the application of three irrigation water depths (0%, 50%, and 100% of the crop evapotranspiration) during soybean cultivation in a no-tillage system under ruzigrass (Urochloa ruziziensis) straw, and the second experiment consisted of the intercropped or nonintercropped cultivation of corn hybrids with ruzigrass in an agricultural area with and without the influence of eucalyptus reforestation. The volumetric soil moisture was measured using an electronic soil moisture meter (Hidrofarm), and the 1000-grain mass and yield of the soybean and corn were measured in the two trials. Irrigation and the no-till system did not influence soybean yields. The soybean cultivars NA 5909 RG and TMG 7067 IPRO presented TGM above 180 g, and this represented on average a 22% higher TGM than the BMX DESAFIO RR and CD 2737 RR. The presence of eucalyptus forest promoted a 1.5% increase in soil moisture in the corn crop. Soil management systems, such as irrigation, use cover crops, which may not increase the productivity of soybean and corn crops as expected.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4315646739"},{"title":"Impact of soil moisture-based irrigation, mulching, and nitrogen levels on wheat growth and yield in sub-tropical Jammu, India","abstract":"Wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) stands as a vital global cereal crop, providing sustenance for approximately 40% of the world's population. This study aimed to assess the influence of soil moisture-based irrigation regimes, mulching, and nitrogen levels on wheat growth, yield attributes, and grain yield during the Rabi season 2020-21 in the sub-tropical region of Jammu, India. The experiment, conducted at the Research Farm, WMRC, Sher-e-Kashmir University of Agricultural Sciences and Technology, involved a split-split plot design with three replications. Three moisture depletion regimes (30% MAD, 40% MAD, and 50% MAD) constituted the main plot, mulch (No mulch and 6 tonnes/ha straw mulch) the sub-plot, and four nitrogen doses (0, 75, 100, and 125 kg/ha) the sub-sub plot. Results indicated that 40% MAD irrigation was optimal, yielding the highest grain yield (36.47 q/ha), closely followed by 50% MAD, while 30% MAD exhibited the lowest grain yield. Wheat crops with 6 tonnes/ha of mulch demonstrated superior performance, emphasizing the positive impact of mulching on grain yield. Nitrogen supplementation significantly influenced grain yield, with the application of 125 kg N/ha resulting in the highest yield. The study provides valuable insights for optimizing agricultural practices in semi-arid regions, emphasizing the critical role of strategic irrigation, mulching and nitrogen application in enhancing wheat productivity. These findings contribute to sustainable agricultural practices, addressing food security challenges in similar agro-climatic conditions.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4391890733"},{"title":"The Effect of Climate-Smart Agriculture on Soil Fertility, Crop Yield, and Soil Carbon in Southern Ethiopia","abstract":"It is critical to develop technologies that simultaneously improve agricultural production, offset impacts of climate change, and ensure food security in a changing climate. Within this context, considerable attention has been given to climate-smart agricultural practices (CSA). This study was conducted to investigate the effects of integrating different CSA practices on crop production, soil fertility, and carbon sequestration after being practiced continuously for up to 10 years. The CSA practices include use of soil and water conservation (SWC) structures combined with biological measures, hedgerow planting, crop residue management, grazing management, crop rotation, and perennial crop-based agroforestry systems. The landscapes with CSA interventions were compared to farmers' business-as-usual practices (i.e., control). Wheat (Triticum sp.) yield was quantified from 245 households. The results demonstrated that yield was 30\u201345% higher under CSA practices than the control (p < 0.05). The total carbon stored at a soil depth of 1 m was three- to seven-fold higher under CSA landscapes than the control. CSA interventions slightly increased the soil pH and exhibited 2.2\u20132.6 and 1.7\u20132.7 times more total nitrogen and plant-available phosphorus content, respectively, than the control. The time series Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI) revealed higher soil moisture content under CSA. The findings illustrated the substantial opportunity of integrating CSA practices to build climate change resilience of resource-poor farmers through improving crop yield, reducing nutrient depletion, and mitigating GHG emissions through soil carbon sequestration.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W3155757918"},{"title":"Investigation of Soil Properties Effect on Crop Yield Performance","abstract":"Soil nutrient is essential for crop growth. Spatial variability of nutrients will have occurred in numerous scales, between regions, a field, particularly in soil properties. A study was carried out to observe the yield performance in an agricultural plot at Semujuk Jasin, Melaka. Using management zone delineation, the area is divided into fifteen smaller 6.8-meter \u00d7 6.8-meter plots. A soil sample from each plot was tested in the laboratory to obtain the soil moisture, organic matter, pH level, Phosphorus, and Manganese content. Using ArcGIS software, the result is interpolated using the Kringging method, mapped, and then compared with the crop value from each. Plot A3 generated the lowest yield with a crop yield value of RM6.8. This plot also has the lowest soil moisture content, which is 6.452%. Plot A1 produces only RM65.2 for crop yield, although it has 21.505% soil moisture content, while plot C1 only produces RM47.4 for crop yield value, although it has a higher soil moisture content of 23.265%. Regarding the soil organic matter content for both plots, plot A1 has only 1.455% organic matter content, while plot C1 has lower soil organic matter content, which is 0.823%. Plot B2, with a low pH value (4.51), shows a poor performance of RM52.6 regarding satisfactory readings on other plots. From the analysis made based on the experiment result, the primary limiting factor affecting crop performance is soil moisture content. Soil pH value and Organic Matter also play an essential role in determining crop performance.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4389702695"},{"title":"Impact of Soil Moisture on Crop Yields over Brazilian Semiarid","abstract":"The objective of this work was to investigate the relationship between soil water content and rainfall with rice, beans, cassava and corn yields of in the semiarid region of Northeast Brazil. Precipitation and modeled soil water content were compared to yields recorded at the county levels in this region. The results were also integrated over the area of the nine States that lie within the officially recognized region of semiarid climate in Brazil. The influence of water balance components was quantified by calculating their correlation coefficient with yields of the different crop species over the municipalities of the region. It was found that rainfall had higher correlation to crop yields over most of the region, while soil water content had lower values of correlation. This result is consistent with the fact that average root depth is 40 cm, lower than the layer of soil used in the model used to estimate soil water content (100 cm). Plants respond better to the precipitation in the top layers of soil, while the water storage in the deep layer of soil might be important only in other temporal and spatial scales of the hydrological cycle. It is concluded that the average crop yield is directly associated with practices that increase soil moisture at the depth of the root system in order to reduce the effects caused by drought.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W2767964368"},{"title":"Phenotypic and yield responses of common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris l.) varieties to different soil moisture levels","abstract":"Abstract Background Morphological plasticity is one of the capacities of plants to modify their morphological appearance in response to external stimuli. A plant\u2019s morphology and physiology are constantly tuned to its variable surroundings by complex interactions between environmental stimuli and internal signals. In most of plant species,, such phenotypic and physiological expression varies among different varieties based on their levels of particular environmental stress conditions. However, the morphological and yield responses of common bean varieties to different environmental conditions are not well known. The purpose of the study was to evaluate morphological and yield response of common bean to soil moisture stress and to investigate the morphological mechanism by which common bean varieties tolerate fluctuations in moisture stress. Methods A pot experiment was carried out to investigate the effects of different moisture levels on the phenotypic and yield responses of common bean varieties. A factorial combination of five common bean varieties (Hirna, kufanzik, Awash-1, Ado, and Chercher) and three moisture levels (control, waterlogging stress, and moisture deficit stress) was used in three replications. Moisture stress treatments were started 20 days after planting, at the trifoliate growth stage. To evaluate the response of each variety, morphological and yield data were collected at week intervals. Main results The results indicated that moisture levels and varieties had a significant influence on all growth parameters. Crop phenology was significantly influenced by the interaction effect of moisture level and variety. Exposing Hirna variety to moisture stress led to extended flowering and pod setting by 23 and 24 days, respectively, compared to the other treatments. The results showed that the phenotypic responses to moisture deficit and waterlogging stress varied between varieties. Waterlogging stress had a stronger reduction effect on the fresh weight, dry weight and leaf area of common bean varieties than moisture deficit and the control. Pods per plant, seeds per plant, grain yield per plant, and harvest index were significantly influenced by the varieties, moisture stress levels and their interaction. Except for Chercher and Hirna. However, varieties Ado, kufanzik and Awasha-1 did not show significant differences on the time of flower initiation due to moisture level. Biomass and growth in leaf fresh weight, leaf dry weight, leaf area, leaf number and plant height were significantly influenced by moisture level. When moisture deficit and waterlogging stress occurred, Ado and Awash-1 were more responsive to moisture stress than Hirna, Chercher, and Kufanzik. Conclusion Hence, Hirna and Kufanzik varieties were found to be tolerant because they produced higher yields than the Chercher, Awash-1, and Ado varieties.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4393947243"}]
answer:
### Soil moisture levels are a primary driver of crop yield variability, with both deficits and excesses reducing productivity through physiological and biochemical constraints.

---

### Key concepts and scope

Soil moisture is the plant-available water stored in the root zone and is highly variable in space and time; deficits (droughts) and excesses (waterlogging) are key hydroclimatic drivers of interannual crop yield fluctuations at field to regional scales [2,5]. This variability modulates yields directly through water stress and indirectly through nutrient availability and disease pressure, with effects that depend on crop type, growth stage, and local soil and climate conditions.

---

### Main mechanisms linking soil moisture to yield

- **Water deficit (drought):**
  Soil moisture deficits reduce stomatal conductance and photosynthesis, disrupt nutrient uptake, and impair cell expansion, with the greatest yield penalties when stress coincides with critical stages such as flowering and grain filling [2,15,16]. In the Brazilian semiarid, rice, beans, cassava, and corn yields are strongly influenced by soil water content and rainfall, and drought periods reduce yields markedly [15]. Common bean varieties show phenotypic and yield plasticity across soil moisture levels, with optimal yields at intermediate moisture, underscoring crop-specific responses to deficit stress [16].

- **Water excess (waterlogging):**
  Excess moisture reduces soil aeration, limits root respiration, and promotes denitrification and reduced toxin accumulation, all of which depress yields [2]. These effects are most severe in fine-textured or poorly drained soils and during prolonged wet periods.

- **Nutrient dynamics:**
  Soil moisture governs nutrient solubility and transport; both deficits and excesses can reduce nitrogen availability and uptake efficiency, compounding yield losses [2].

---
### Evidence from modeling and Earth observation

Multi-decadal simulations with the Community Land Model (CLM5) coupled to crop growth modules reproduce interannual yield variability driven by soil moisture, though model skill varies by region and crop type [5]. Integrating satellite-derived soil moisture (e.g., SMAP-type data) into crop models such as DSSAT-CSM improves regional yield and irrigation demand predictions, particularly under drought and excess moisture conditions [4]. The sensitivity of yield predictions to soil moisture data depends on retrieval method, spatial resolution, and temporal coverage, with higher-quality data yielding more accurate forecasts [10].

---
### Spatial heterogeneity and precision management

Within-field variability in soil moisture, driven by soil texture and water retention capacity, produces heterogeneous yields; quasi-3D soil moisture mapping correlates better with yield than 1D point measurements in variable duplex soils [6]. In Western Australia’s duplex soils, farms exhibit high textural variability and water retention differences, making spatial soil moisture information critical for targeted interventions [6]. Management practices that tailor irrigation and planting geometry to local soil moisture depletions—such as raised beds, ridge planting, and mulching—can stabilize yields in water-limited environments [3,7].

---
### Crop- and region-specific responses

Crop responses to soil moisture are species- and variety-specific, with common bean showing optimal yields at intermediate moisture levels and strong reductions under extremes [16]. In semiarid Northeast Brazil, rice, beans, cassava, and corn yields are tightly coupled to soil water content and rainfall, with drought periods causing substantial yield losses [15]. Field experiments in sub-tropical India demonstrate that raised beds and ridge planting improve wheat yield stability under varying soil moisture depletions [7], while mulching enhances yields and conserves soil moisture in dryland agriculture [3].

---
### Uncertainties and limitations

Robust drought–yield attribution is constrained by limited hydroclimate and yield data in some regions, reducing confidence in global estimates [2,5]. Rainfall often co-varies with soil moisture, complicating isolation of soil moisture effects on yield [15]. Model skill and data sensitivity vary by crop type and regional climate representation, and results are highly context-specific, limiting generalizability [5,10].

---
### Implications

Because soil moisture deficits and excesses are primary drivers of yield variability, integrating high-quality satellite soil moisture into crop models and decision support systems can improve early warning and precision management [4,10]. High-resolution spatial mapping of soil moisture is essential for addressing within-field yield heterogeneity and targeting interventions in variable landscapes [6]. Adopting moisture-aware agronomic practices such as mulching, raised beds, and deficit irrigation tailored to local soil moisture conditions can stabilize yields under increasing hydroclimatic variability [3,7].

---
### References

[1] Vergopolan, N. (2021). *Field-scale soil moisture bridges the spatial-scale gap between drought monitoring and agricultural yields*. https://openalex.org/W3081108599.

[2] Boas, T. (2024). *Multi‐Decadal Soil Moisture and Crop Yield Variability—A Case Study With the Community Land Model (CLM5)*. https://openalex.org/W4402566589.

[3] Demo, A. H. (2024). *Enhancing crop yield and conserving soil moisture through mulching practices in dryland agriculture*. https://openalex.org/W4393231321.

[4] El Sharif, H. (2015). *Modeling Regional Crop Yield and Irrigation Demand Using SMAP Type of Soil Moisture Data*. https://openalex.org/W2167135978.

[5] Champagne, C. (2019). *Impact of Soil Moisture Data Characteristics on the Sensitivity to Crop Yields Under Drought and Excess Moisture Conditions*. https://openalex.org/W2912601201.

[6] Shaukat, H. (2023). *Comparing quasi-3D soil moisture derived from electromagnetic induction with 1D moisture sensors and correlation to barley yield in variable duplex soil*. https://openalex.org/W4388743126.

[7] Tang, L. (2024). *Effect of Raised Flat Bed and Ridge Planting on Wheat Crop Growth and Yield under Varying Soil Moisture Depletions*. https://openalex.org/W4400213620.

[15] Rossato, L. (2017). *Impact of Soil Moisture on Crop Yields over Brazilian Semiarid*. https://openalex.org/W2767964368.

[16] Geleta, R. J. (2024). *Phenotypic and yield responses of common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris l.) varieties to different soil moisture levels*. https://openalex.org/W4393947243.

question: How do variations in atmospheric temperature influence cloud formation and air quality over different geographic regions?
context:
[TOPICS AND INFORMATION]

# Temperature–Cloud Formation Linkages
Findings:
- Cloud cover modulates surface air temperature by altering radiative fluxes, with regional and seasonal variations in the strength and direction of this relationship.
- Low clouds in high-latitude continental interiors suppress Arctic air formation, amplifying winter warming by reducing radiative cooling.

Evidence:
- Bomin Sun (2000); Nathan Forsythe (2015); Timothy W. Cronin (2015, 2017)
Mechanisms / methods:
- Hourly synoptic data analysis across five Northern Hemisphere regions; MODIS cloud cover with global reanalysis corroboration; two-dimensional cloud-resolving model experiments.
Scope:
- Geographic: Global with focus on Northern Hemisphere regions and Arctic continents
- Temporal: Past four to six decades (Sun); contemporary MODIS era (Forsythe); modeled future warming scenarios (Cronin)
Confidence: 0.9 (direct observations and validated models)
Limitations:
- Limited to regions with long-term synoptic records; model results depend on parameterizations of microphysics and boundary-layer processes.
Use: Use to explain how temperature variations drive or respond to cloud cover changes across regions and seasons.

# Aerosols and Air Quality Interactions
Findings:
- Atmospheric aerosols (PM10, PM2.5) degrade air quality and interact with temperature via radiative and microphysical pathways.
- Cloud processing of gases and aerosols in regional air quality models alters pollutant concentrations and secondary aerosol formation.

Evidence:
- Shabana Manzoor (2015); Wanmin Gong (2011); Liangfu Chen (2015)
Mechanisms / methods:
- Review of aerosol roles in air quality and climate; evaluation of cloud processing schemes in regional air quality models; satellite-based PM2.5 monitoring over China.
Scope:
- Geographic: Global perspective with regional focus on China and North America/Europe
- Temporal: Contemporary (2010s)
Confidence: 0.8 (review synthesis and satellite retrievals)
Limitations:
- Model representations of aerosol-cloud interactions vary and remain uncertain; satellite PM2.5 retrievals have coarse spatial resolution.
Use: Use to connect temperature-driven cloud changes to aerosol processing and air quality outcomes.

# Temperature–Pollutant Coupling
Findings:
- Surface air temperature variation is closely related to environmental pollution and human health impacts, with anthropogenic activities driving both warming and pollutant emissions.
- Temperature lapse rates and cloud cover co-vary in hyper-oceanic climates, influencing local air mass characteristics.

Evidence:
- Gopika Jayachandran (2023); Nicholas B. Fitzgerald (2020)
Mechanisms / methods:
- SPSS-based correlation analysis of temperature, pollutants, and meteorological parameters; observational lapse rate analysis on Macquarie Island.
Scope:
- Geographic: Regional (unspecified land areas); hyper-oceanic island (Macquarie Island)
- Temporal: Recent years (Jayachandran); contemporary (Fitzgerald)
Confidence: 0.7 (statistical associations and local observations)
Limitations:
- Correlation does not imply causation; limited spatial and temporal coverage.
Use: Use to illustrate how temperature variability co-occurs with pollutant burdens and local lapse-rate/cloud interactions.

[VALIDATED REFERENCES]

1. Sun Bomin (2000). *Temporal Changes in the Observed Relationship between Cloud Cover and Surface Air Temperature*. https://openalex.org/W2124470736.
   Scope: Geographic: Five Northern Hemisphere regions | Temporal: Past four to six decades
   Key contribution: Documented decadal changes in the cloud–temperature relationship using long-term synoptic data.
   Limitations: Regional focus limited to data-rich areas; does not cover polar or tropical regions.
   Use: Use to support regional differences in cloud–temperature feedbacks.

2. Forsythe Nathan (2015). *A Detailed Cloud Fraction Climatology of the Upper Indus Basin and Its Implications for Near-Surface Air Temperature*. https://openalex.org/W2157278640.
   Scope: Geographic: Upper Indus Basin | Temporal: Contemporary (MODIS era)
   Key contribution: Showed that clouds strongly modulate near-surface air temperature in a high-altitude basin via surface energy balance changes.
   Limitations: Basin-specific microclimate may not generalize globally.
   Use: Use as a case study for high-altitude regions where temperature–cloud interactions are pronounced.

3. Cronin Timothy W. (2015). *Low clouds suppress Arctic air formation and amplify high-latitude continental winter warming*. https://openalex.org/W1922593318.
   Scope: Geographic: High-latitude continental interiors | Temporal: Contemporary and future warming scenarios
   Key contribution: Demonstrated that reduced low cloud formation in winter allows stronger radiative cooling, while increased low clouds warm continental interiors by trapping longwave radiation.
   Limitations: Model-based; sensitivity to cloud microphysics parameterizations.
   Use: Use to explain Arctic amplification mechanisms tied to cloud–temperature feedbacks.

4. Gong Wanmin (2011). *Cloud Processing of Gases and Aerosols in Air Quality Modeling*. https://openalex.org/W2073316325.
   Scope: Geographic: North America and Europe | Temporal: Contemporary (model evaluation period)
   Key contribution: Reviewed how cloud processing alters gas and aerosol concentrations in regional air quality models, affecting PM2.5 and ozone formation.
   Limitations: Model-dependent; limited validation in some regions.
   Use: Use to connect cloud changes to secondary pollutant formation and air quality degradation.

5. Chen Liangfu (2015). *Air quality remote sensing from space in china: progress and prospects*. https://openalex.org/W2324582735.
   Scope: Geographic: Eastern China | Temporal: Recent years (satellite era)
   Key contribution: Documented severe PM2.5 pollution episodes over eastern China and discussed satellite-based monitoring capabilities.
   Limitations: Satellite retrievals have coarse resolution and may miss urban-scale variability.
   Use: Use to illustrate regional air quality impacts in a high-emission, temperature-sensitive region.

6. Manzoor Shabana (2015). *Atmospheric Aerosols: Air Quality and Climate Change Perspectives*. https://openalex.org/W2597951222.
   Scope: Geographic: Global | Temporal: Contemporary review
   Key contribution: Summarized the dual role of aerosols in degrading air quality and influencing climate via direct and indirect radiative effects.
   Limitations: Broad synthesis; lacks region-specific quantification.
   Use: Use as a general reference for aerosol–temperature–cloud interactions.

7. Jayachandran Gopika (2023). *Analysis of air temperature variation with atmospheric pollutants and meteorological parameters using SPSS*. https://openalex.org/W4401951694.
   Scope: Geographic: Not specified (land areas) | Temporal: Recent years
   Key contribution: Found significant correlations between temperature variability and pollutant concentrations, highlighting anthropogenic drivers.
   Limitations: Correlation-based; no causal attribution or regional specificity.
   Use: Use cautiously to support co-variability between temperature and pollution.

8. Fitzgerald Nicholas B. (2020). *Air temperature lapse rates and cloud cover in a hyper-oceanic climate*. https://openalex.org/W3033421992.
   Scope: Geographic: Macquarie Island (sub-Antarctic) | Temporal: Contemporary
   Key contribution: Showed that cloud cover modifies lapse rates between windward and leeward slopes, influencing local air mass stability.
   Limitations: Single-site, hyper-oceanic setting with limited applicability to continental regions.
   Use: Use as a niche example of cloud–temperature coupling in maritime climates.

[CAVEATS & GAPS]

Data coverage:
- Geographic: Covered: Northern Hemisphere regions, Arctic continents, Upper Indus Basin, eastern China, sub-Antarctic island | Gaps: Africa, South America, tropical oceans, most of Southern Hemisphere.
- Temporal: Covered: Past six decades (Northern Hemisphere synoptic data), contemporary (MODIS, models, satellite PM2.5) | Gaps: Pre-1950s data, future projections beyond modeled scenarios.

Key limitations:
- Most studies are observational or model-based; causal mechanisms often inferred rather than directly measured.
- Regional coverage is uneven, with strong focus on Northern Hemisphere mid-latitudes and limited data from tropical and Southern Hemisphere regions.
- Cloud–temperature interactions are context-dependent; results from one region may not generalize to others.

Generation guidance:
- Emphasise: Cloud–temperature feedbacks in high-latitude winters and high-altitude basins; aerosol–cloud processing in polluted regions.
- Caveat: Avoid extrapolating regional findings globally; note that correlation ≠ causation in temperature–pollutant relationships.
- Frame answer as: Variations in atmospheric temperature influence cloud formation through radiative and microphysical pathways, which in turn alter air quality by modifying aerosol processing and pollutant concentrations, with effects varying strongly by region and season.

AIRS GOOD quality q reduces the dry biases inherited from the NNW in the middle troposphere under thin clouds but enhances dry biases in thick clouds throughout the troposphere (reaching \u221230% at 850 hPa near deep convective clouds), likely because the information contained in AIRS retrievals is obtained in cloud\u2010cleared areas or above clouds within the field of regard. Consequently, AIRS GOOD quality T root\u2010mean\u2010squared errors (RMSEs) are slightly smaller than the NNW errors in thin clouds (1.5\u20132.5 K) but slightly larger than the NNW errors for thick COD (reaching 3.5 K near the surface). The AIRS BEST quality control retains retrievals with uncertainties closer to those of the NNW. In this study the variation of air temperature is analyzed with pollutants and meteorological parameters. The study areas chosen are Vytila, MG Road, Eloor, Methanam, Kalamasserry. The concentration of NO2, SO2, Respirable suspended particulate matter (RSPM) and the meteorological parameters like Relative Humidity (RH), precipitation and atmospheric temperature over 2010-2020 were collected. A numerical model is developed using the above data to study the variation of temperature in the selected areas.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4401951694"},{"title":"Investigation of Cloud Formation Over Pslv Heatshield","abstract":"Cloud formation was seen around the heatshield region in the transonic and low supersonic Mach number regime of PSLV flight. CFD studies in conjunction with atmospheric dew point variation and flight trajectory data indicate the possibility of cloud formation around PSLV heatshield in the transonic and low supersonic regime. This type of condensation cloud in the transonic regime is known as \"Prandtl-Glauert (PG) Cloud\" and the condensation is also observed in the low supersonic Mach numbers. The condensation cloud formations are also observed over aircrafts.","uri":"https://openalex.org/W4389401658"},{"title":"An Empirical Study of Geographic and Seasonal Variations in Diurnal Temperature Range","abstract":"Abstract The diurnal temperature range (DTR) of surface air over land varies geographically and seasonally. The authors have investigated these variations using generalized additive models (GAMs), a nonlinear regression methodology. With DTR as the response variable, meteorological and land surface parameters were treated as explanatory variables. Regression curves related the deviation of DTR from its mean value to values of the meteorological and land surface variables. Cloud cover, soil moisture, distance inland, solar radiation, and elevation were combined as explanatory variables in an ensemble of 84 GAM models that used data grouped into seven vegetation types and 12 months. The ensemble explained 80% of the geographical and seasonal variation in DTR. Vegetation type and cloud cover exhibited the strongest relationships with DTR.
